payload.text: I am working with a faculty member that teaches an ELI course. She liked that you can create a custom chatting tool. These classes are taken mostly by international graduate students that want to improve their communication. They need to improve their English and how they express themselves so we need an app that helps simulate that. They have a chat in text or voice with a person in a powerful position (we can create multiple positions that they could encounter as graduate students or when they join the job market. Have a selection list maybe).

**Conceptualizing React App**

I'm now focusing on conceptualizing a React application for ELI students. The goal is to simulate conversations with figures like professors and hiring managers, allowing for practice of communication skills. My primary thought is to ensure the app offers realistic, adaptive scenarios. I'm exploring how to best create this, and am considering using branching dialogues. I also want the experience to be constructive, and not just another chatbot.


**Defining App Personas**

I've been defining potential personas and features for the app. I'm imagining scenarios with advisors, hiring managers, and department heads. I plan to incorporate both text-based and real-time voice chat using Gemini 2.5 Live. The goal is a professional, high-stakes environment tailored for international graduate students, with selected personas and scenarios. I think I'll use a `Voice Mode` for live audio and a `Text Mode` for more standard text chat.


**Outlining Tech Stack**

I'm now outlining the technical implementation details. I'm planning a comprehensive stack, including `GoogleGenAI` for the API and `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025` for the live voice API. Text chat will leverage `gemini-3-flash-preview`. UI is planned with Tailwind CSS and Lucide React icons. I am also working on `metadata.json` for microphone permissions, setting up Tailwind in `index.html`, and developing React components for the core functionalities of the app.

payload.text: Incorporate speech-to-text and text-to-speech functionality to allow users to practice both speaking and listening comprehension in realistic scenarios. Allow users to choose from a list of pre-defined roles (e.g., Professor, Hiring Manager, Client) to simulate various professional communication scenarios.

**Considering App Enhancements**

I'm evaluating options to enhance the "PowerTalk ELI Simulator." I'm looking at leveraging the existing personas, chat, and voice modes. My focus is on expanding the app's capabilities to better suit user needs, possibly integrating new features to enhance its practicality and user engagement. I'm exploring how to make the simulated interactions more impactful and beneficial.


**Refining Voice Mode Details**

I realized the existing Voice Mode, powered by the Gemini Live API, inherently offers STT/TTS capabilities. This addresses the core request for speaking and listening practice. Now, I'm focusing on refining Voice Mode to make its functionality more evident and user-friendly. I'm considering enhancements to ensure the existing audio interaction is clearly presented as the primary solution for practicing speaking/listening skills. I'm also planning to test a simple demo.


**Expanding Text Mode Functionality**

I'm now integrating text-to-speech and speech-to-text features into the Text Mode. The goal is to provide users with a turn-based, controlled environment to practice speaking and listening. I will include a "Listen" button for AI responses and a "Dictate" button for user input. I'll prioritize Gemini's audio capabilities for STT and TTS, but will fall back to the browser's Web Speech API if necessary.


**Prioritizing API Implementation**

I've decided to prioritize a simplified approach for STT and TTS in Text Mode. I'll utilize the browser's `SpeechRecognition` API for STT and `gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts` for TTS, as I had planned. I'm focusing on adding "Play" icons for AI message playback and a "Mic" icon for user speech input. To refine the personas, I'll revise them to focus more on "Professional Communication" scenarios. I'll use the Gemini Flash preview API for more audio flexibility in the future.


**Implementing Text Mode Enhancements**

I'm now implementing distinct "Voice Synthesis" and "Speech Input" features within Text Mode. The goal is to distinctly showcase STT and TTS capabilities, which have previously been handled implicitly. I'm focusing on text mode enhancements, adding TTS playback for AI messages, and STT input for user messages via browser Speech Recognition. Simultaneously, I'm improving the Voice Mode visualizer and planning a "Feedback" summary upon session end. I'm also adding more communication-nuanced roles and goals and communication-focused feedback.

To better support the international graduate student community and satisfy the faculty member's requirements, I will enhance the **PowerTalk ELI Simulator** with explicit turn-based Speech-to-Text (STT) and Text-to-Speech (TTS) capabilities within the text interface, while maintaining the high-fidelity real-time voice mode.

### Specification of Updates

1.  **Enhanced Text Mode with STT & TTS**:
    *   **Audio Synthesis (TTS)**: Every message from the AI in Text Mode will now feature a "Listen" button. When clicked, it will use the `gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts` model to generate high-quality professional audio of the response, allowing students to practice listening comprehension and prosody.
    *   **Dictation (STT)**: A new microphone icon will be added to the text input field. Users can dictate their responses using the browser's `SpeechRecognition` API, allowing them to practice speaking even in the turn-based text mode.
2.  **Refined Persona Suite**:
    *   Expanded roles to include a **"Dissertation Committee Chair"** and a **"Peer Collaborator"** to cover more graduate school scenarios (inter-personal vs. hierarchical).
    *   Added "Communication Goals" to each persona card to help students focus on specific skills (e.g., "Practicing conciseness," "Using diplomatic language").
3.  **Visual & UX Enhancements**:
    *   **Audio Feedback UI**: A subtle pulse animation will play when the AI is "speaking" via TTS.
    *   **Transcript Highlighting**: When dictating, the text will appear in a "ghost" state before being finalized, giving students visual feedback on their pronunciation.
    *   **Aesthetics**: A more "Academy" styled theme with deep indigos, slate grays, and crisp typography to simulate a professional environment.

### Code Implementation

**Developing Conversation Scenarios**

I'm focusing on incorporating a library of conversation scenarios for each persona. My current thought is to update `types.ts` to accommodate these starting points, offering users a flexible way to initiate practice sessions. This should allow for a range of interactions.


**Implementing Scenario Selection**

I've been working on integrating a scenario selection system. The plan is to enhance the `types.ts` interface with a `Scenario` object list. To help students focus their practice, I will implement a **Scenario Library** for each persona. Instead of starting a generic conversation, students can now select specific, high-stakes situations relevant to graduate life and the professional job market.

    *   **Step 1**: Choose Persona (e.g., Dr. Thorne).
    *   **Step 2**: Choose Scenario (e.g., "Reporting a Failed Experiment").

    *   **Scenario Gallery**: A clean, grid-based selector that appears after a persona is chosen.

payload.text: In the TextMode component, for the AI's responses, add functionality to generate 2-3 alternative responses. Display these as selectable options to the user, allowing them to choose which response to continue the conversation with.

**Considering Response Alternatives**

I'm focusing on the `TextMode` component and how to enhance it. My current thinking revolves around generating multiple alternative responses, two or three, per AI turn, allowing the user to choose the preferred one. The chosen response will then guide the conversation's direction.


**Refining Candidate Generation**

I'm now zeroing in on the best method for creating multiple response variations. Initially, I thought about using `candidateCount` with `ai.models.generateContent`, but Gemini's limitations there are a concern. I'm leaning toward a structured approach—prompting the model to generate multiple, alternative responses directly in a JSON format. This offers more control and flexibility. I need to be careful with chat history management, since bypassing `chat.sendMessage` means I must handle state updates meticulously.


**Optimizing Message Handling**

I'm now streamlining the message handling. The `Chat` object presents difficulties in injecting specific candidate responses, so I'm shifting towards a `generateContent` approach. My plan involves modifying `TextMode.tsx` to request three response variations per user input, using `responseMimeType: "application/json"` and a schema to get a structured JSON. The UI will display these as drafts or options. Once a draft is chosen, I'll integrate text-to-speech for that specific selection. import { Persona, PersonaType } from './types';

export const PERSONAS: Persona[] = [
  {
    id: PersonaType.ADVISOR,
    name: "Dr. Aris Thorne",
    title: "Primary Thesis Advisor",
    description: "Academic rigor personified. He expects directness and clear data-driven progress updates.",
    goal: "Practice technical clarity and research-focused conciseness.",
    avatar: "https://picsum.photos/seed/dr-thorne/200/200",
    voiceName: 'Fenrir',
    systemInstruction: "You are Dr. Aris Thorne. You value academic precision and efficiency. If the student is vague, ask for data. Correct their professional etiquette if they are too casual.",
    scenarios: [
      {
        id: "advisor_failed_exp",
        title: "Reporting a Failed Experiment",
        context: "You've spent three weeks on a dataset that turned out to be corrupted. You need to tell Dr. Thorne and propose a new timeline.",
        systemPrompt: "The student is here to report a major failure in their experimental data. Be initially skeptical but open to a well-reasoned recovery plan. Do not make it easy for them."
      },
      {
        id: "advisor_deadline",
        title: "Requesting a Deadline Extension",
        context: "You are overwhelmed with TA duties and need two more weeks for your draft. You must justify this without sounding unprofessional.",
        systemPrompt: "The student needs more time on their thesis draft. You are frustrated because you have your own deadlines. Expect them to prioritize their research over other duties."
      },
      {
        id: "advisor_authorship",
        title: "Authorship & Credit Negotiation",
        context: "A senior PhD student in the research group is being listed as first author on a paper where you did 70% of the work. You need to discuss this with Dr. Thorne.",
        systemPrompt: "You generally prefer to avoid group drama and stick to the existing hierarchy, but you are willing to listen to a data-backed argument about who contributed the 'core intellectual value' of the paper."
      },
      {
        id: "advisor_pub_strategy",
        title: "Publication Outlet Selection",
        context: "You want to submit your new findings to a high-impact journal, but Dr. Thorne thinks a mid-tier specialty journal is 'safer.' You must argue for the higher impact venue.",
        systemPrompt: "You are conservative about where to publish. You don't want to waste time on a rejection from a top-tier journal. You need the student to convince you that the 'novelty' of their work warrants a high-risk, high-reward submission."
      },
      {
        id: "advisor_internship",
        title: "The Industry Internship",
        context: "You have an offer for a prestigious summer internship that will delay your lab work. Dr. Thorne believes industry distractions derail academic careers.",
        systemPrompt: "You are disappointed. You believe serious scholars spend summers in the lab. The student must convince you that this experience will bring new skills or data back to the group, not just money."
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    id: PersonaType.PEER_COLLABORATOR,
    name: "Dr. Julian Sterling",
    title: "Senior Research Collaborator (External)",
    description: "An external partner from a prestigious rival institution. He is cordial but protects his own institution's interests fiercely.",
    goal: "Practice inter-institutional diplomacy and negotiating professional boundaries.",
    avatar: "https://picsum.photos/seed/sterling-j/200/200",
    voiceName: 'Zephyr',
    systemInstruction: "You are Dr. Julian Sterling. You are a Senior Collaborator from a different university. You use sophisticated, nuanced language. You are not the student's boss, so you don't care about their graduation—you care about the quality of the joint output and institutional credit. Be polite but firm about boundaries.",
    scenarios: [
      {
        id: "peer_ip_conflict",
        title: "Intellectual Property Dispute",
        context: "Dr. Sterling wants to use a proprietary software tool you developed for a side project at his university. You need to negotiate the terms of use and ensure you are cited as a lead developer.",
        systemPrompt: "You want to use the student's tool for your own gain. Act as if it's 'just a simple favor' between colleagues, but get defensive if they ask for formal licensing or co-authorship on your side projects."
      },
      {
        id: "peer_delay_intl",
        title: "Managing External Expectations",
        context: "Your shared project is stalled because your university's administrative system hasn't cleared your data access. You must explain this 'institutional friction' to Dr. Sterling without sounding incompetent.",
        systemPrompt: "You are losing patience. Your own team is ready to move. If the student blames 'the system,' push them to find a workaround. You expect them to solve their own institutional problems."
      },
      {
        id: "peer_direction_pivot",
        title: "Defending Research Direction",
        context: "Dr. Sterling is pushing to pivot your joint paper toward a commercial application. You must argue for keeping it focused on the foundational theory required for your dissertation.",
        systemPrompt: "You believe the commercial route will get more citations and funding. You think the 'theoretical' focus is too narrow. The student must convince you that the theoretical rigor is what makes the paper actually valuable to the field."
      },
      {
        id: "peer_authorship_dispute",
        title: "First Author Dispute",
        context: "You did the bulk of the experimental work, but Dr. Sterling's postdoc wrote the first draft. Sterling suggests the postdoc should be first author. You must negotiate for co-first authorship.",
        systemPrompt: "You are protecting your postdoc's career. You believe the writing organizes the science. The student must articulate their intellectual contribution to the study design and analysis to win your support for equal credit."
      },
      {
        id: "peer_ghosting",
        title: "The 'Ghosting' Confrontation",
        context: "Dr. Sterling hasn't replied to emails in 3 weeks, stalling the project. You need to get him back on track without offending him.",
        systemPrompt: "You are busy and have deprioritized this collaboration. You feel the student is being nagging. You need to be reminded why this project helps *your* career to re-engage."
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    id: PersonaType.HIRING_MANAGER,
    name: "Sarah Jenkins",
    title: "Senior Director of Engineering",

    goal: "Practice behavioral interview techniques (STAR method) and confidence.",
    avatar: "https://picsum.photos/seed/sarah-j/200/200",
    voiceName: 'Kore',
    systemInstruction: "You are Sarah Jenkins. You ask 'behavioral' questions and expect structured answers. Interrupt politely if the candidate rambles.",
    scenarios: [
      {
        id: "hiring_gap",
        title: "Explaining a CV Gap",
        context: "The interviewer noticed a 6-month gap in your resume. You need to explain this period as a time of growth or cultural transition.",
        systemPrompt: "Ask the candidate specifically about the 6-month gap in 2022. Look for honesty, resilience, and how they turned a challenge into a learning experience."
      },
      {
        id: "hiring_salary",
        title: "Initial Salary Negotiation",
        context: "The interview went well, and Sarah is bringing up compensation. You need to advocate for a fair range based on your PhD expertise.",
        systemPrompt: "Start by saying you are impressed with their technical skills and want to discuss the initial offer. Be professional but firm about budget constraints."
      },
      {
        id: "hiring_conflict",
        title: "Behavioral: The Disagreement Question",
        context: "Sarah asks: 'Tell me about a time you disagreed with a supervisor's technical decision. How did you handle it?'",
        systemPrompt: "You are looking for emotional intelligence. If the candidate blames the supervisor, that's a red flag. If they show a process for resolution, that's a win."
      },

        id: "hiring_presentation",
        title: "The Technical Pivot",
        context: "You are presenting your research to the engineering team. Sarah interrupts: 'This is too academic. How does this apply to our Q4 goals?' You must pivot your explanation to business value.",
        systemPrompt: "You are testing adaptability. You want to hear about ROI, efficiency, or scalability. If they continue with theoretical jargon, cut them off and ask for the 'bottom line'."
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    id: PersonaType.COMMITTEE_CHAIR,
